content:
# Acts Chapter 17 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 246-247 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_017_page_001.jpg, ACT_017_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now having traveled through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews.

**2** And according to his custom, Paul went in to them, and for three Sabbaths he reasoned with them from the Scriptures,

**3** opening and setting forth that it was necessary for the Christ to suffer and to rise from the dead, and that "This is the Christ, Jesus, whom I proclaim to you."

**4** And some of them were persuaded and were allotted to Paul and Silas, and of the God-fearing Greeks a great multitude, and of the prominent women not a few.

**5** But the Jews, being jealous, and having taken certain wicked men of the marketplace, and having formed a mob, threw the city into an uproar; and having come upon the house of Jason, they were seeking to bring them out to the people.

**6** And not having found them, they dragged Jason and certain brothers before the city rulers, crying out, "These men who have turned the inhabited world upside down have come here also,

**7** whom Jason has received; and these all act contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying there is another king, Jesus."

**8** And they troubled the crowd and the city rulers when they heard these things.

**9** And having taken a security bond from Jason and the rest, they released them.

**10** And the brothers immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea, who, having arrived, went into the synagogue of the Jews.

**11** Now these were more noble-minded than those in Thessalonica, who received the word with all eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily to see whether these things were so.

**12** Therefore many of them believed, and of the prominent Greek women and men not a few.

**13** But when the Jews from Thessalonica learned that the word of God was proclaimed by Paul in Berea also, they came there as well, stirring up and troubling the crowds.

**14** And then immediately the brothers sent Paul away to go as far as the sea, but both Silas and Timothy remained there.

**15** And those who conducted Paul brought him as far as Athens; and having received a command for Silas and Timothy to come to him as quickly as possible, they departed.

**16** Now while Paul was waiting for them in Athens, his spirit was provoked within him as he saw the city full of idols.

**17** Therefore he was reasoning in the synagogue with the Jews and the God-fearing ones, and in the marketplace every day with those who happened to be present.

**18** And also some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers were conversing with him. And some were saying, "What might this seed-picker wish to say?" And others, "He seems to be a proclaimer of foreign divinities" -- because he was proclaiming Jesus and the resurrection.

**19** And having taken hold of him, they brought him to the Areopagus, saying, "Are we able to know what this new teaching is that is being spoken by you?

**20** For you are bringing certain strange things to our ears. We wish therefore to know what these things mean."

**21** Now all the Athenians and the foreigners residing there spent their time in nothing other than telling or hearing something newer.

**22** And Paul, standing in the midst of the Areopagus, said: "Men of Athens, I observe that in all things you are very religious.

**23** For passing through and observing the objects of your worship, I found also an altar on which had been inscribed, 'To an Unknown God.' What therefore you worship in ignorance, this I proclaim to you.

**24** The God who made the world and all the things in it, he being Lord of heaven and earth, does not dwell in temples made with hands,

**25** nor is he served by human hands as though he needed anything, since he himself gives to all life and breath and all things.

**26** And he made from one every nation of men to dwell upon all the face of the earth, having determined their appointed times and the boundaries of their habitation,

**27** to seek God, if perhaps they might grope for him and find him, though indeed he is not far from each one of us.

**28** For in him we live and move and have our being, as also some of your own poets have said, 'For we are indeed his offspring.'

**29** "Being therefore the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Divine is like gold or silver or stone, a graven thing of the craft and imagination of man.

**30** Therefore, having overlooked the times of ignorance, God now commands all men everywhere to repent,

**31** because he has fixed a day in which he is about to judge the inhabited world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed, having furnished proof to all by raising him from the dead."

**32** Now when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some began to mock, but others said, "We will hear you again concerning this."

**33** Thus Paul went out from their midst.

**34** But certain men, having joined themselves to him, believed, among whom also were Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 17 is among the most intellectually consequential chapters in the New Testament. It narrates three distinct episodes -- mob violence in Thessalonica (vv. 1-9), the noble reception in Berea (vv. 10-15), and Paul's address at the Areopagus in Athens (vv. 16-34) -- each of which reveals a different mode of response to the gospel. The Areopagus speech (vv. 22-31) is the only extended address to a purely pagan audience in Acts and represents Paul's most philosophically sophisticated rhetorical performance, engaging Stoic and Epicurean categories while subverting them with distinctly Jewish-Christian claims about creation, judgment, and resurrection.

**Manuscript condition:** The text of Acts 17 begins on page 246 (image 001), continuing from chapter 16 in the preceding columns. The column markers at the bottom of page 246 indicate Acts 17:5 and 17:11, confirming that the Thessalonica and Berea narratives span the right-hand columns. The text continues onto page 247 (image 002), with markers at 17:17, 17:23, and 17:30, confirming that the Areopagus speech occupies the central columns of this page and the chapter concludes before Acts 18:3 in the final column. The text is well preserved in the primary hand (scribe A) with clear scriptio continua throughout. Standard nomina sacra are visible: QS/QU (God), KS/KU (Lord), XS/XU (Christ). No significant corrector interventions are visible in this chapter. Key readings confirmed by visual inspection: PROSEKLHRWQHSAN (v. 4), ZHLOSANTES (v. 5), ANASTATWSANTES (v. 6), EUGENESTEROI (v. 11), SPERMOLOGOS (v. 18), DEISIDAIMONESTEROUS (v. 22), AGNWSTW QEW (v. 23), EX ENOS without AIMATOS (v. 26), PSELAFHSEIAN (v. 27), TOU GAR KAI GENOS ESMEN (v. 28), DIONUSIOS O AREOPAGEITHS (v. 34).

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show strong agreement across this chapter, particularly in the Areopagus speech where the elevated Greek naturally produces similar English renderings. All three correctly preserve: "seed-picker" for spermologos (v. 18), "from one" without "blood" (v. 26), the Aratus quotation (v. 28), and the identification of Dionysius as "the Areopagite" (v. 34). Primary points of divergence involve: (1) proseklerothesan -- "were allotted" vs. "joined themselves" vs. "threw in their lot" (v. 4), (2) deisidaimonesterous -- "more religious than others" vs. "very religious" vs. "extremely religious" (v. 22), (3) rendering of the Areopagus speech's opening address (v. 22), (4) "inhabited world" vs. "world" for oikoumene (vv. 6, 31), (5) degree of dynamism in the mob scene (v. 5), and (6) treatment of the "Unknown God" inscription (v. 23).

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as its formal equivalence approach balances the elevated register of the Areopagus speech with clear English prose. EMET's literal renderings are consulted for morphological precision, particularly for the distinctive Sinaiticus reading proseklerothesan (v. 4) and the compressed "from one" (v. 26). PNEUMA's dynamic readings contribute clarity in the narrative sections (vv. 1-15) but tend toward excessive paraphrase in the speech (vv. 22-31), where the Greek's own rhetorical force requires preservation rather than adaptation.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Verse 4 -- "were allotted" (proseklerothesan):** This is the distinctive Sinaiticus reading. EMET renders "were-allotted"; LOGOS renders "joined themselves"; PNEUMA renders "threw in their lot." SOPHIA follows EMET: "were allotted" preserves the specific Sinaiticus verb proseklerothesan (a passive form meaning "were assigned by lot"), which is the lectio difficilior. The Byzantine reading prosethesan ("were added") is simpler, and both LOGOS and PNEUMA appear to have smoothed toward it. EMET correctly flags this as the distinctive Alexandrian reading. The passive voice is theologically suggestive: they were allotted to Paul and Silas, implying divine agency in the allocation. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

2. **Verse 5 -- "the Jews, being jealous":** All three translators correctly follow the Sinaiticus reading, which names "the Jews" as subject and includes the motivation of jealousy (zelos) without the later manuscript addition "who did not believe" (hoi apeithountes). SOPHIA preserves the shorter Sinaiticus reading. For the marketplace men: EMET renders "marketplace-loungers"; LOGOS renders "wicked men of the marketplace"; PNEUMA renders "thugs from the marketplace." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "wicked men of the marketplace" -- the Greek agoraioi andres means men who loiter in the agora, available for hire; "wicked" (poneroi) is in the text and must be rendered; "thugs" (PNEUMA) captures the menace but is colloquial.

3. **Verse 6 -- "turned the inhabited world upside down" (ten oikoumenen anastatosantes):** EMET renders "having upset the inhabited world"; LOGOS renders "turned the world upside down"; PNEUMA renders "turned the whole world upside down." SOPHIA renders "turned the inhabited world upside down" -- a composite drawing "inhabited world" from EMET (preserving oikoumene's specific meaning as the Roman-administered world, not the planet) and "turned upside down" from LOGOS/PNEUMA (preserving the proverbial English phrase rooted in this very passage). PNEUMA's addition of "whole" is not in the Greek.

4. **Verse 11 -- "more noble-minded" (eugenesteroi):** EMET renders "more noble"; LOGOS renders "more noble-minded"; PNEUMA renders "more open-minded." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "more noble-minded" -- the comparative eugenesteroi literally means "more well-born" but Luke's context makes clear that the nobility is intellectual and moral rather than social. LOGOS's "noble-minded" captures the dispositional sense without PNEUMA's departure from the word's semantic range (the Bereans are not merely "open-minded" in the modern liberal sense; they are noble in their method of testing claims against Scripture).

5. **Verse 18 -- "seed-picker" (spermologos):** All three translators preserve "seed-picker" rather than smoothing to "babbler." This is a unanimous decision. The Athenian slang term -- literally "one who picks up seeds" like a bird in the marketplace, applied contemptuously to intellectual scavengers -- is vivid, culturally specific, and confirmed by the manuscript reading SPERMOLOGOS on page 247. SOPHIA preserves it without alteration.

6. **Verse 21 -- "something newer" (kainoteron):** EMET renders "something newer"; LOGOS renders "something new"; PNEUMA renders "the latest ideas." SOPHIA follows EMET: "something newer" -- the Greek kainoteron is a comparative form, not merely "new" but "newer" (i.e., newer than the last new thing). This nuance captures the Athenian obsession with novelty as an escalating appetite. LOGOS's positive form loses the comparative; PNEUMA's "latest ideas" is a paraphrase.

7. **Verse 22 -- "very religious" (deisidaimonesterous):** EMET renders "more-religious-than-others"; LOGOS renders "very religious"; PNEUMA renders "extremely religious." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "very religious" -- the Greek deisidaimonesterous is a superlative/elative form of deisidaimon, which carries deliberate ambiguity between "very religious" and "very superstitious." Paul's rhetorical skill lies in using a word that simultaneously compliments and critiques. "Very religious" preserves this ambiguity in English -- it can be heard as a genuine acknowledgment or as a subtle barb. EMET's "more-religious-than-others" is morphologically precise (comparative form) but awkward; PNEUMA's "extremely religious" pushes too far toward compliment and loses the edge. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

8. **Verse 23 -- "To an Unknown God" (Agnostw Theow):** All three translators render the inscription correctly. The Sinaiticus reading is singular (AGNWSTW QEW), confirmed by the manuscript on page 247. Historical sources (Pausanias, Diogenes Laertius) attest to plural inscriptions ("to unknown gods") in Athens; Paul's use of the singular is theologically purposeful, enabling his identification of this "unknown" deity with the God who made the world. SOPHIA preserves the singular as attested.

9. **Verse 26 -- "from one" (ex henos):** EMET renders "from one"; LOGOS renders "from one"; PNEUMA renders "from one man." All three correctly note that Sinaiticus reads EX ENOS without the noun haimatos ("blood") found in later manuscripts. SOPHIA renders "from one" -- follows EMET/LOGOS against PNEUMA's interpretive addition of "man." The Greek leaves the referent open: "from one [man]" or "from one [origin]." While the implied referent is likely Adam, the manuscript's compressed reading should not be expanded. The Byzantine addition of "one blood" (ex henos haimatos) is a scribal clarification not present in Sinaiticus. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

10. **Verse 28 -- Aratus quotation:** All three translators correctly render "For we are indeed his offspring" (tou gar kai genos esmen), identified as a quotation from the Cilician poet Aratus (Phaenomena 5) and possibly Cleanthes (Hymn to Zeus). Paul, a native of Tarsus in Cilicia, would have known Aratus well. The quotation demonstrates Paul's strategy of establishing common ground with his philosophical audience before redirecting them toward the distinctively Christian claims of judgment and resurrection.

11. **Verse 30 -- "having overlooked" (hyperidoon):** EMET renders "having-overlooked"; LOGOS renders "having overlooked"; PNEUMA renders "God overlooked." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "having overlooked" preserves the aorist participial construction that subordinates God's past overlooking to his present command. The verb hyperidoon (from hyperorao, "to look over/past") indicates a deliberate decision not to act, now rescinded by the universal command to repent. This is not casual tolerance but sovereign forbearance terminated by eschatological urgency.

12. **Verse 31 -- "the inhabited world" vs. "the world":** EMET renders "the inhabited world" (ten oikoumenen); LOGOS renders "the world"; PNEUMA renders "the world." SOPHIA renders "the inhabited world" -- follows EMET against LOGOS/PNEUMA, consistent with the decision in v. 6. The Greek oikoumene specifically denotes the inhabited/civilized world (the Roman oecumene), not the physical planet (kosmos, used in v. 24). Paul uses kosmos in v. 24 for God's creative work and oikoumene in v. 31 for God's judicial work -- the distinction is deliberate and should be preserved.

13. **Verse 34 -- "Dionysius the Areopagite":** All three translators preserve the identification. EMET and LOGOS render "Dionysius the Areopagite"; PNEUMA renders "Dionysius, a member of the Areopagus." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "Dionysius the Areopagite" -- the Greek DIONUSIOS O AREOPAGEITHS is a title, not a description. "The Areopagite" (ho Areopagites) designates membership in the Areopagus council, Athens's most prestigious judicial and philosophical body. PNEUMA's "a member of the Areopagus" is accurate but converts the Greek title into a description; the title itself should be preserved. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Opening conjunction | "And having-traveled-through" | "Now having traveled through" | No conjunction (begins with "They") | "Now having traveled through" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek has no strong conjunction; LOGOS's "Now" serves as a transitional marker |
| 4 | "were allotted" vs. "joined themselves" vs. "threw in their lot" | "were-allotted" (proseklerothesan) | "joined themselves" | "threw in their lot" | **"were allotted"** -- follows EMET (1-to-2 override); preserves the distinctive Sinaiticus passive reading; see detailed entry below |
| 4 | "God-fearing Greeks" vs. "devout Greeks" vs. "God-fearing Greeks" | "God-fearing Greeks" | "devout Greeks" | "God-fearing Greeks" | "God-fearing Greeks" -- follows EMET/PNEUMA (2-to-1); sebomenon specifically means "God-fearing/worshiping," a recognized term for Gentile synagogue adherents |
| 5 | "marketplace-loungers" vs. "wicked men of the marketplace" vs. "thugs from the marketplace" | "marketplace-loungers" (agoraious) | "wicked men of the marketplace" | "thugs from the marketplace" | "wicked men of the marketplace" -- follows LOGOS; preserves both ponerous (wicked) and agoraious (of the marketplace) |
| 5 | "having-formed-a-mob" vs. "having gathered a crowd" vs. "formed a mob" | "having-formed-a-mob" (ochlopoiesantes) | "having gathered a crowd" | "formed a mob" | "having formed a mob" -- composite; ochlopoieo means specifically to mob-make, not merely gather a crowd; EMET's compound is accurate but needs the hyphen removed; LOGOS undertranslates the menace |
| 6 | "the inhabited world" vs. "the world" vs. "the whole world" | "the inhabited-[world]" (oikoumenen) | "the world" | "the whole world" | "the inhabited world" -- follows EMET; oikoumene specifically means the inhabited/Roman world; PNEUMA's "whole" is not in the Greek |
| 11 | "more noble" vs. "more noble-minded" vs. "more open-minded" | "more-noble" (eugenesteroi) | "more noble-minded" | "more open-minded" | "more noble-minded" -- follows LOGOS; captures the dispositional sense of eugenes |
| 17 | "God-fearing ones" vs. "devout persons" vs. "God-fearing Greeks" | "the God-fearing-[ones]" | "the devout persons" | "the God-fearing Greeks" | "the God-fearing ones" -- follows EMET; the Greek tois sebomenois does not specify "Greeks" here (PNEUMA adds); "God-fearing" is the recognized term |
| 18 | "were-encountering" vs. "were conversing" vs. "began sparring" | "were-encountering" (syneballon) | "were conversing" | "began sparring" | "were conversing" -- follows LOGOS; synballo means to converse/discuss/engage; "sparring" (PNEUMA) is adversarial beyond the Greek; "encountering" (EMET) is too physical |
| 21 | "something newer" vs. "something new" vs. "the latest ideas" | "something newer" (kainoteron) | "something new" | "the latest ideas" | "something newer" -- follows EMET; the comparative form kainoteron must be preserved |
| **22** | **deisidaimonesterous rendering** | "more-religious-than-others" | "very religious" | "extremely religious" | **"very religious"** -- follows LOGOS; preserves the deliberate ambiguity between "religious" and "superstitious"; see detailed entry below |
| 23 | "What therefore you being ignorant worship" vs. "What therefore you worship in ignorance" vs. "What you worship without knowing" | "What therefore [you] being-ignorant worship" | "What therefore you worship in ignorance" | "What you worship without knowing" | "What therefore you worship in ignorance" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the oun (therefore) and renders agnoountes as a state rather than an action |
| **26** | **"from one" with or without "man"** | "from one" (ex henos) | "from one" | "from one man" | **"from one"** -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); the manuscript reads EX ENOS without a supplied noun; see detailed entry below |
| 26 | "the dwelling of them" vs. "their habitation" vs. "where they would live" | "the boundaries of-the dwelling of-them" | "the boundaries of their habitation" | "the boundaries of where they would live" | "the boundaries of their habitation" -- follows LOGOS; katoikias rendered as "habitation" |
| 29 | "an engraving of art" vs. "a graven thing of the craft" vs. "an image shaped by human craft" | "[an] engraving of-art and imagination of-man" | "a graven thing of the craft and imagination of man" | "an image shaped by human craft and imagination" | "a graven thing of the craft and imagination of man" -- follows LOGOS; charagmati technes preserved as "graven thing of the craft" |
| 30 | "men, all everywhere" vs. "all men everywhere" vs. "all people everywhere" | "men, all everywhere" | "all men everywhere" | "all people everywhere" | "all men everywhere" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek anthropois pantas pantachou uses anthropos (men/humans); PNEUMA's "people" is acceptable but LOGOS preserves the Greek term |
| **31** | **"the inhabited world" vs. "the world"** | "the inhabited-[world]" (oikoumenen) | "the world" | "the world" | **"the inhabited world"** -- follows EMET (1-to-2 override); consistent with v. 6; oikoumene is distinct from kosmos (v. 24) |
| 32 | "were-mocking" vs. "began to mock" vs. "sneered" | "were-mocking" (echleuazon) | "began to mock" | "sneered" | "began to mock" -- follows LOGOS; the imperfect echleuazon suggests the onset of mocking; "sneered" (PNEUMA) narrows the Greek |
| 33 | "went out from the midst of them" vs. "went out from their midst" vs. "left the council" | "went-out from [the] midst of-them" | "went out from their midst" | "left the council" | "went out from their midst" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "left the council" interprets beyond the Greek |
| **34** | **"the Areopagite" vs. "a member of the Areopagus"** | "Dionysius the Areopagite" | "Dionysius the Areopagite" | "Dionysius, a member of the Areopagus" | **"Dionysius the Areopagite"** -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); preserves the Greek title ho Areopagites; see detailed entry below |

-- SOPHIA
